theorem_statement: Let \(K\) be a field of characteristic zero, and let \(u : \mathbb N \to K\) be a sequence satisfying a linear recurrence with constant coefficients over \(K\). Then the zero set \(Z(u)=\{n\in\mathbb N : u_n=0\}\) is the union of a finite set and finitely many arithmetic progressions.
raw_proof_text:
We prove the theorem by reducing a linear recurrence to exponential-polynomial functions and then analyzing the zero sets on residue classes.

Let the recurrence have order \(r\), so that
\[
u_{n+r}+c_{r-1}u_{n+r-1}+\cdots+c_0u_n=0
\]
for all sufficiently large \(n\). Replacing the first finitely many terms by part of the finite exceptional set, we may assume the recurrence holds for every \(n\ge 0\). Let
\[
P(T)=T^r+c_{r-1}T^{r-1}+\cdots+c_0
\]
be the characteristic polynomial. After passing to a finite extension field, write
\[
P(T)=\prod_{j=1}^s (T-\lambda_j)^{m_j}.
\]
Standard linear recurrence theory gives an expression
\[
u_n=\sum_{j=1}^s Q_j(n)\lambda_j^n,
\]
where each \(Q_j\) is a polynomial of degree less than \(m_j\). This follows by observing that the sequences \(n^a\lambda_j^n\), with \(0\le a<m_j\), form a basis for the solution space of the recurrence after the characteristic polynomial is split.

It remains to understand the zero set of such an exponential polynomial. Put the nonzero characteristic roots into equivalence classes by declaring
\[
\lambda_i\sim \lambda_j
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\lambda_i/\lambda_j\text{ is a root of unity}.
\]
Choose a positive integer \(M\) such that every root-of-unity ratio occurring among the \(\lambda_j\)'s has order dividing \(M\). For each residue class \(a\in\{0,1,\ldots,M-1\}\), define the subsequence
\[
v_m=u_{a+Mm}.
\]
Then
\[
v_m=\sum_j Q_j(a+Mm)\lambda_j^a(\lambda_j^M)^m.
\]
On a fixed residue class, all roots that differed by a root of unity now have the same \(M\)-th power, while roots from different equivalence classes have quotients that are not roots of unity. Thus the zero set of \(u\) is a finite union of residue-class zero sets. If one of these residue-class subsequences vanishes identically, it contributes the arithmetic progression \(a+M\mathbb N\). It is therefore enough to prove that any non-identically-zero nondegenerate exponential polynomial has only finitely many zeros, where nondegenerate means that no quotient of two distinct exponential bases is a root of unity.

We now prove the needed nondegenerate case. Let
\[
v_m=\sum_{j=1}^s R_j(m)\mu_j^m
\]
be nondegenerate and not identically zero. The coefficients and bases lie in a finitely generated field of characteristic zero. Choose a prime \(p\) and embed this field into a finite extension of \(\mathbb Q_p\) in such a way that the finitely many nonzero numbers involved are defined in the \(p\)-adic field. After replacing \(m\) by residue classes modulo a further integer \(q\), we may assume that each \(\mu_j^q\) is sufficiently close to \(1\) in the \(p\)-adic norm for the \(p\)-adic logarithm and exponential to define \((\mu_j^q)^z\) analytically for \(z\in\mathbb Z_p\).

For a fixed residue class \(b\) modulo \(q\), define a \(p\)-adic analytic function
\[
F_b(z)=\sum_{j=1}^s R_j(b+qz)\mu_j^b(\mu_j^q)^z.
\]
For every nonnegative integer \(\ell\), we have
\[
F_b(\ell)=v_{b+q\ell}.
\]
If the subsequence \(v_{b+q\ell}\) has infinitely many zeros, then \(F_b\) has infinitely many zeros in the compact set \(\mathbb Z_p\). By the \(p\)-adic zero-isolation theorem for analytic functions, a nonzero \(p\)-adic analytic function has only finitely many zeros in \(\mathbb Z_p\). Hence \(F_b\) must be identically zero.

If \(F_b\) is identically zero, then all integer values \(v_{b+q\ell}\) vanish. This contributes the full arithmetic progression \(b+q\mathbb N\) inside the previously chosen residue class. Otherwise, the zero set in that residue class is finite. Since there are only finitely many residue classes, the total zero set is a finite union of arithmetic progressions together with a finite exceptional set.

Finally, passing to a finite extension field did not change which original terms \(u_n\) are zero: an element of \(K\) is zero in \(K\) if and only if it is zero after extension of scalars. Therefore the same finite union description holds for the original sequence over \(K\). This proves the theorem.
